nativeRange.habitat: Reef environments at moderate to deeper depths (8–150 m), particularly along seaward drop-offs and current-swept ledges with rich invertebrate growth

diet.notes: Has been documented eating zooplankton, copepods, meaty crustaceans, and small fish in the wild. In captivity tends to accept squid, krill, clams, hard-shelled shrimp, and small fish. Regularly offering hard-shelled foods is generally advised to help wear down continuously growing teeth.

goodWith: other semi-aggressive fish of similar or larger size

summary: The Bluelined Triggerfish (Xanthichthys auromarginatus) is generally considered one of the least aggressive triggerfish, making it more approachable for reef aquariums than most relatives. Males are easily identified by a vivid blue throat patch absent in females.
careNotes:
The Bluelined Triggerfish, also widely sold as the Blue Throat or Bluechin Triggerfish, is generally considered one of the least aggressive members of the triggerfish family, making it more suitable for community reef or fish-only setups than most of its relatives. Males are easily identified by a bright blue throat and jaw patch and blue-lined face markings; females lack this coloration and tend to be noticeably duller overall. Even so, most aquarium keepers treat this species as reef-safe only with caution, as individuals have been known to prey on small ornamental shrimp and other small crustaceans.

In the wild, this species tends to form loose aggregations a few meters above the reef, frequenting current-swept seaward drop-offs and ledges in the Indo-Pacific. In aquaria, individuals have been known to rearrange live rock and sand, so securing rockwork is advisable. One male can generally be housed with one or more females without issue; keeping multiple males together is generally not advised, as aggression between males has been reported. Females are typically smaller and less colorful than males.

This species has been reported to spit water out of the tank to attract feeding attention from its keeper. Hand-feeding is possible, but biting has been observed, so feeding with tongs is commonly recommended. Like all triggerfish, it possesses strong, lockable dorsal spines used defensively; these spines are not venomous.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has rarely been reported. In the wild, the species is oviparous (egg-laying) and forms stable mated pairs. In aquaria, one male can generally be housed with one or more females; keeping multiple males is not commonly advised. Detailed captive breeding protocols remain poorly documented in the aquarium literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Collection page confirmed species listing; species-specific product page (liveaquaria.com/product/233) returned 404 at time of access. Care level (easy) and temperament (aggressive) noted from collection listing; these were averaged with secondary sources.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 in / 30 cm), tank size (75 gal / 284 L), temperament (semi-aggressive), diet, reef safety, and native range. Water parameters not listed on this page.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, family (Balistidae), order (Tetraodontiformes), max size (30 cm), native range (Indo-Pacific from Mauritius to Hawaii/Japan/Australia), temperature range (18.3–28.2°C), and diet (zooplankton/copepods). Used as authoritative taxonomic and size reference.

additional.name: Reef Chasers
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size ~9 in (~23 cm typical aquarium), minimum tank 125 gal (473 L), semi-reef-safe status (may prey on small invertebrates), carnivore diet. Listed temperament as generally peaceful — averaged with other sources to semi-aggressive.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 30 cm, salinity ~1.026, diet (meaty/crustaceans), taxonomy (Balistidae). Tank size recommendation (1,500 L) was treated as an outlier and excluded from averaging; it likely represents an absolute maximum rather than a hobbyist minimum.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely documented. The species is oviparous with distinct pairing; mated pairs can be housed together. Multiple males in one tank are generally not advised.
